name: Zion National Park
heroCaption: Zion Canyon — Photo: National Park Service

alerts.title: Flash Floods
alerts.message: Flash Floods are the #1 killer in Zion slot canyons. Check daily flash flood potential at the Visitor Center. The Narrows closes during warnings + 2 hours after. If water rises or turns muddy: get to high ground immediately.

alerts.title: Angels Landing Closure
alerts.message: Maintenance closure April 20–23, 2026. No permits issued for these dates.

alerts.title: Limited Cell Service
alerts.message: Cell service is very limited in the canyon. Download offline maps, screenshot your permits, and tell someone your hiking plan before you go.

trails.name: Angels Landing
trails.description: One of America's most famous hikes. Final 0.5mi along narrow spine with 1,000ft drops and chain handrails. Walter's Wiggles = 21 switchbacks. Scout Lookout (no permit needed) still has great views.
tips: Best time: Early morning (first shuttle). Avoid midday congestion on the chains.
Pro tip: Bring gloves for the chain section. No trekking poles past Scout Lookout.
Don't go if: Wet, icy, or thunderstorms — sandstone becomes dangerously slick.
Permit: $6 application + $3/person. Apply via seasonal or day-before lottery.

trails.name: The Narrows (Bottom-Up)
trails.description: Iconic slot canyon — 1,000ft walls only 20–30ft apart. Hike IN the Virgin River. Go as far as you want. Wall Street section (~1.5mi in) is the narrowest and most dramatic. Rent canyon boots.
tips: Best time: Late May–June or late Sep–Oct. Check river flow before going.
Pro tip: Rent canyoneering boots — rocks are like "greased bowling balls." $25–32/day.
Wall Street (~1.5 mi in) is the narrowest, most dramatic section — great turnaround point.
Safety: Never go if flow >150 CFS or flash flood warnings are active.

trails.name: Observation Point (East Mesa)
trails.description: Arguably the best viewpoint in Zion — 2,148ft above the canyon floor. Look DOWN on Angels Landing. Original Weeping Rock trail closed since 2019. Access via East Mesa Trail (unpaved road, high-clearance recommended). Arrive by 7 AM.
tips: Best time: Sunrise for photographers. Arrive by 7 AM for parking.
Pro tip: Better views than Angels Landing, no permit, no chains, no exposure.
Access: Unpaved road to trailhead — high-clearance vehicle recommended.
Note: Original Weeping Rock access closed since 2019. Use East Mesa Trail only.

trails.name: Canyon Overlook Trail
trails.description: Best bang-for-buck short hike. Stunning panoramic canyon views. Just east of the Zion–Mt. Carmel Tunnel. Parking is very limited — arrive at sunrise. Accessible by private vehicle only.
tips: Best time: Sunrise or sunset for stunning panoramic light on the canyon.
Pro tip: Best bang-for-buck short hike in Zion. Parking is very limited — arrive at dawn.
Access: Private vehicle only — just east of the Zion–Mt. Carmel Tunnel. No shuttle.
Great for: Families, photographers, anyone short on time.

trails.name: Watchman Trail
trails.description: Underrated gem. Great sunset views of the Watchman, Towers of the Virgin, and Springdale. Starts near the Visitor Center. Sandy, rocky terrain with some moderate drop-offs.
tips: Best time: Late afternoon/sunset. Golden light on the Watchman is stunning.
Pro tip: Underrated and rarely crowded. Great views of Towers of the Virgin and Springdale.
Terrain: Sandy and rocky with some moderate drop-offs. Wear sturdy shoes.
Bonus: Starts near the Visitor Center — easy to fit in before/after other activities.

trails.name: Pa'rus Trail
trails.description: Paved, flat trail along the Virgin River. The ONLY trail in Zion that allows dogs. Great for stargazing at night. Bikes allowed. Connects Visitor Center to Canyon Junction.
tips: Best for: Families, dogs (only dog-friendly trail in Zion), wheelchairs, bikes.
Pro tip: Exceptional stargazing at night — Zion is Dark Sky Certified.
Connects: Visitor Center to Canyon Junction. Flat and paved the entire way.
Bonus: Great for an early morning or evening stroll along the Virgin River.

trails.name: Riverside Walk
trails.description: Paved trail along the Virgin River leading to the mouth of The Narrows. Hanging gardens and narrowing canyon walls. This is where The Narrows hike begins.
tips: Best for: Families, wheelchair users (paved), anyone headed to The Narrows.
Pro tip: This trail leads directly to the mouth of The Narrows — combine them.
Highlights: Hanging gardens, narrowing canyon walls, Virgin River views.
Note: Very popular. Start early to avoid crowds.

trails.name: Emerald Pools (Lower/Middle/Upper)
trails.description: Walk behind a waterfall at Lower Pool. Upper Pool has a dramatic high waterfall. Can combine all three as a loop. Best during spring runoff for impressive water flow.
tips: Best time: Spring for impressive waterfall flow. Lower Pool has a walk-behind waterfall.
Pro tip: Combine all three pools as a loop for the full experience (~3 mi total).
Upper Pool has the most dramatic waterfall but steepest access.
Note: Trail can be slippery when wet. Watch footing near the pools.

trails.name: The Subway
trails.description: Technical canyoneering route through a tube-shaped canyon. 80 permits/day. Top-down (9.5 mi one way) requires rappelling gear; bottom-up (9 mi RT) is scrambling. Kolob Terrace Road access. Bring a dry suit for pool crossings.
tips: Permit: Required — 80 permits/day. Apply on recreation.gov.
Top-down: 9.5 mi one way, requires rappelling gear and canyoneering experience.
Bottom-up: 9 mi RT, strenuous scrambling but no technical gear needed.
Pro tip: Bring a dry suit for pool crossings. Water is cold year-round.

trails.name: The Narrows (Top-Down)
trails.description: 16-mile through-hike from Chamberlain's Ranch. Mostly downhill in the river. Can be day-hiked (10–14 hrs) or overnighted (12 campsites). Requires shuttle to trailhead.
tips: Permit: Required. Apply via recreation.gov lottery 2 days before (midnight–3 PM MT).
Pro tip: Mostly downhill in the river. Can day-hike (10–14 hrs) or overnight (12 campsites).
Logistics: Requires shuttle to Chamberlain's Ranch — outfitters provide this.
Gear: Full canyoneering rental + dry bag for overnight gear.

trails.name: West Rim Trail
trails.description: Some of the best backcountry viewpoints with far fewer people than Angels Landing. Three reliable water sources along the route. Top-to-bottom route.
tips: Best for: Experienced hikers wanting backcountry solitude with incredible views.
Pro tip: Top-to-bottom route. Three reliable water sources along the way.
Permit: Free for day hikes. Overnight requires wilderness permit.
Access: Start from Kolob Terrace Road (Lava Point) for the downhill direction.

trails.name: Kolob Arch (La Verkin Creek)
trails.description: Views of one of the world's largest natural arches (287ft span). Trail follows Timber and La Verkin Creeks through riparian areas. Located in the Kolob Canyons section (separate I-15 entrance).
tips: Highlight: One of the world's largest natural arches (287ft span).
Pro tip: Combine with Hop Valley Trail for a backpacking loop.
Location: Kolob Canyons section — separate I-15 entrance, far fewer crowds.
Water: La Verkin Creek is reliable but treat/filter before drinking.

trails.name: Taylor Creek Trail (Kolob)
trails.description: Most popular Kolob trail. Follows a small creek to Double Arch Alcove. Two historic 1930s homestead cabins along the way. Far fewer crowds than main canyon.
tips: Best for: A taste of Kolob without a huge commitment. Most popular Kolob trail.
Highlights: Two 1930s homestead cabins and Double Arch Alcove at the end.
Pro tip: Far fewer crowds than main canyon. Great fall colors in October.
Note: Several creek crossings — waterproof boots helpful in spring.

trails.name: Timber Creek Overlook (Kolob)
trails.description: "Most overlooked trail in Zion." Ridge walk with panoramic Kolob Canyon and Pine Valley Mountain views. At the end of Kolob Canyons Road.
tips: Best for: Quick panoramic views with minimal effort. Families and casual visitors.
Pro tip: At the very end of Kolob Canyons Road. Combine with the scenic drive.
Views: Panoramic Kolob Canyon and Pine Valley Mountain vistas.
Time: Only 30 minutes round trip — no excuse to skip it.

trails.name: Weeping Rock
trails.description: Short, steep paved trail to an alcove with dripping springs and hanging gardens. Water seeps through 4,000 years of sandstone. Reopened Sep 2025 after rockfall closure.
tips: Best for: A quick stop — great for families and anyone between shuttle rides.
Highlight: Water seeps through 4,000 years of sandstone into a lush hanging garden.
Pro tip: Reopened Sep 2025 after years of rockfall closure. Short but steep.
Combine with: A stop at Big Bend (stop 8) for canyon views on the same shuttle ride.

trails.name: Kayenta Trail
trails.description: Connector trail between the Grotto and Upper Emerald Pool. Unpaved with moderate grades and some drop-offs. Creates a great loop with Emerald Pools trails. Canyon views along the way.
tips: Best for: Creating a loop with Emerald Pools trails for a richer experience.
Pro tip: Unpaved with moderate grades and some drop-offs — more scenic than the Grotto Trail.
Connects: The Grotto (stop 6) to Upper Emerald Pool area.
Views: Beautiful canyon vistas along the traverse.

trails.name: Sand Bench Trail
trails.description: Sandy loop above the canyon floor with views of the Streaked Wall and the Sentinel. Shared with horseback riders. Can be accessed from Court of the Patriarchs or Zion Lodge.
tips: Best for: A quieter moderate hike. Shared with horseback riders.
Pro tip: Sandy terrain — wear gaiters or shoes that handle sand well.
Views: Streaked Wall, the Sentinel, and broad canyon panoramas.
Access: Court of the Patriarchs (stop 4) or Zion Lodge (stop 5).

trails.name: Archaeology Trail
trails.description: Very short interpretive trail near the Visitor Center. Views of an ancient Ancestral Puebloan storage site and the Towers of the Virgin. Great for kids or a quick stop.
tips: Best for: Kids, history buffs, or a quick 20-min stop near the Visitor Center.
Highlight: Views of an ancient Ancestral Puebloan grain storage site.
Pro tip: Great warm-up trail while waiting for shuttle lines to shorten.
Combine with: Walk to Pa'rus Trail or Watchman Trail from here.

trails.name: Grotto Trail
trails.description: Short paved connector between Zion Lodge and the Grotto picnic area. Follows the Virgin River. Good for stretching legs between shuttle stops.
tips: Best for: Connecting Zion Lodge to The Grotto without riding the shuttle.
Pro tip: Flat, paved, and along the Virgin River. Pleasant but not a destination hike.
Combine with: Use as a connector between Emerald Pools and Angels Landing trailheads.
Time: About 30 minutes — good for stretching legs.

trails.name: Hidden Canyon
trails.description: Narrow slot canyon with chains, hanging gardens, and a natural arch. Closed since 2019 rockfall. No confirmed reopening date. Formerly accessed from Weeping Rock trailhead.
tips: Status: Closed since 2019 due to rockfall. No confirmed reopening date.
When open: A narrow slot canyon with chains, hanging gardens, and a natural arch.
Access: Formerly from Weeping Rock trailhead (stop 7).
Note: Do not attempt to access — trail is physically blocked.

trails.name: East Rim Trail (Full)
trails.description: Point-to-point trail from the east entrance to Weeping Rock. Passes through Echo Canyon, slickrock, and forested plateaus. Top section still accessible. Lower section closed since 2019 rockfall.
tips: Status: Lower section (Weeping Rock end) closed since 2019 rockfall.
Upper section: Still accessible from east entrance. Scenic slickrock and forest.
Pro tip: Can be combined with East Mesa Trail to reach Observation Point.
Permit: Free for day hikes. Overnight requires wilderness permit.

trails.name: Northgate Peaks Trail
trails.description: Hidden gem on Kolob Terrace Road. Views of the two Northgate Peaks framing a deep valley. Flat mesa walk to a dramatic viewpoint. Very few people.
tips: Best for: Dramatic views with very few people. Hidden gem on Kolob Terrace Road.
Pro tip: Mesa walk to a jaw-dropping viewpoint between two towering peaks. ~5.9 mi RT with ~400ft gain.
Access: Kolob Terrace Road (starts in Virgin, UT). Closed in winter.
Combine with: Wildcat Canyon or Connector Trail for a longer day.

trails.name: Hop Valley Trail
trails.description: Wide valley trail through meadows and ponderosa pines. Connects Kolob Terrace Road to La Verkin Creek/Kolob Arch. Sandy terrain. Used as connector for loop routes.
tips: Best for: Backpackers connecting to La Verkin Creek / Kolob Arch.
Pro tip: Wide meadow valley with ponderosa pines. Sandy — feels longer than it is.
Access: Kolob Terrace Road trailhead.
Permit: Free for day hikes. Overnight requires wilderness permit.

trails.name: Wildcat Canyon Trail
trails.description: Connects Kolob Terrace Road to the West Rim Trail via Wildcat Canyon. Forested plateau with meadows. Can be combined with Northgate Peaks and Connector trails for a long day hike or backpacking loop.
tips: Best for: Backpackers and strong hikers wanting solitude on Zion's plateau.
Pro tip: Connects to West Rim Trail for multi-day loop options.
Terrain: Forested plateau with meadows. Less dramatic but very peaceful.
Access: Kolob Terrace Road. Closed in winter.

trails.name: Cable Mountain Trail
trails.description: Historic trail to remains of an aerial tramway used to transport lumber 1901–1926. Views straight down into Zion Canyon. Access via Stave Spring trailhead (unpaved road).
tips: Highlight: Remains of a historic aerial tramway (1901–1926) at the rim.
Pro tip: Views straight down into Zion Canyon. Very few visitors.
Access: Via Stave Spring trailhead (unpaved road, high-clearance recommended).
Combine with: Deertrap Mountain Trail from the same trailhead.

trails.name: Deertrap Mountain Trail
trails.description: Lightly traveled trail through ponderosa forest ending at a canyon rim viewpoint. Shares trailhead with Cable Mountain via Stave Spring. Solitude guaranteed.
tips: Best for: Absolute solitude. One of Zion's least-traveled trails.
Pro tip: Ponderosa forest ending at a canyon rim viewpoint. Peaceful and quiet.
Access: Shares Stave Spring trailhead with Cable Mountain Trail.
Note: Unpaved road to trailhead — high-clearance vehicle recommended.

trails.name: Chinle Trail
trails.description: Desert trail through petrified wood deposits and Chinle Formation geology. Connects to Coalpits Wash. Trailhead off Anasazi Plateau near Rockville. Hot in summer, best Nov–Mar.
tips: Best time: Nov–Mar. Extremely hot in summer — no shade.
Highlight: Petrified wood deposits and unique Chinle Formation geology.
Pro tip: Connects to Coalpits Wash for a longer desert loop.
Access: Trailhead off Anasazi Plateau near Rockville.

trails.name: Coalpits Wash
trails.description: Remote desert wash hike in Zion's least-visited SW corner. Follows a dry wash through desert scrub. Can loop with Chinle Trail. Petrified wood and fossils. Very hot May–Sep.
tips: Best time: Nov–Mar. Desert wash — no shade, very hot May–Sep.
Highlight: Petrified wood, fossils, and Zion's least-visited corner.
Pro tip: Can loop with Chinle Trail for a full desert experience.
Note: Bring all water — no reliable sources on trail.

trails.name: Many Pools
trails.description: Unofficial but well-known trail on the east side. Sculpted sandstone with seasonal tiered pools. Best after rain. No maintained trail — route-finding on slickrock. Near Mt. Carmel Hwy.
tips: Best time: After rain — the tiered pools fill up with water for a magical scene.
Pro tip: Unofficial trail on slickrock. Route-finding skills needed. No maintained path.
Access: Pullout on Mt. Carmel Highway (east side of park).
Note: Can be dry and unimpressive without recent rain. Check conditions.

uniqueSections.title: The Narrows
uniqueSections.heroCaption: The Narrows — Hiking through the Virgin River — Photo: NPS

flowWarning.message: Always check river flow before going. Below 50 CFS = easy. 50–70 CFS = moderate. 70–150 CFS = challenging. Above 150 CFS = CLOSED.

bottomUp.title: Bottom-Up (No Permit)
bottomUp.description: Walk the 1-mile Riverside Walk, then enter the river. Go as far as you want.

milestones.name: Big Spring
milestones.note: farthest point without permit, 10 mi RT

topDown.title: Top-Down Through-Hike (Permit Required)
topDown.route: Chamberlain's Ranch to Temple of Sinawava
topDown.permitTiming: Apply 2 days before, midnight–3 PM MT

essentials: Canyoneering boots — rocks are like "greased bowling balls"
Neoprene socks — water ranges 38–65°F depending on season
Sturdy walking stick (NOT collapsible — they fail in fast water)
coldSeasonAddOns: Dry bibs — waterproof with latex ankle gaskets (spring/fall)
Full dry suit — Kokotat with wrist/ankle gaskets (winter)

uniqueSections.cyanobacteriaWarning: Toxic cyanobacteria detected in the Virgin River. NEVER drink river water — no filter can remove the toxins. Get drinking water from side springs only, then filter/disinfect. A dog has died from exposure.

uniqueSections.title: Angels Landing
uniqueSections.heroCaption: Angels Landing — Chain section with 1,000ft drops — Photo: NPS

details: First 2 mi: switchback trail through Refrigerator Canyon
Walter's Wiggles: 21 tight switchbacks to Scout Lookout
Scout Lookout: Excellent views, no permit needed here
Final 0.5 mi: Narrow spine, chains, 1,000ft+ drops both sides
Bring gloves for chain section (better grip)
Do NOT hike if wet or icy — sandstone becomes extremely slippery
Trekking poles NOT recommended on chain section

permit.description: Required for any portion past Scout Lookout. $6 application + $3/person if selected. Max group: 6.

dayBeforeLottery.applyWindow: 12:01 AM – 3:00 PM MT the day before
dayBeforeLottery.note: Surprisingly successful if you're flexible with start time.

uniqueSections.title: Shuttle System
uniqueSections.frequency: Every 5–10 min (Canyon line), 10–15 min (Springdale line). Private vehicles prohibited in canyon during shuttle season.

canyonStops.name: Weeping Rock
canyonStops.info: short trail reopened Sep 2025 (East Rim / Hidden Canyon still closed)

canyonStops.name: Big Bend
canyonStops.note: southbound only — no stop heading up-canyon

springdaleStops.name: Hotel De Novo
springdaleStops.info: West end of Springdale. Formerly Majestic View Lodge.

springdaleStops.name: Hampton Inn / Hyatt Place / Bit & Spur
Zion Canyon Village
springdaleStops.info: Narrows gear rental, Restaurants. Walk into park. Connects to Canyon Line

uniqueSections.eBikeTip: Rent e-bikes in Springdale to bypass shuttle lines entirely. Cycle the 6-mile Scenic Drive at your own pace with 360° views.
uniqueSections.shuttleRules: No food, smoking, or pets on shuttles (service animals excepted).

insiderTips.title: Arrive before 8 AM or skip the car entirely.
insiderTips.detail: Park lots fill by 8:00–8:30 AM. Park in Springdale and take the free town shuttle, or rent e-bikes to bypass lines completely.

insiderTips.title: Visit late October for ideal conditions.
insiderTips.detail: The fall colors are peaking, temps are in the 70s, and the summer families have long since gone home. Alternatively, winter for solitude + self-driving access.

insiderTips.title: In summer, start at 5 AM.
insiderTips.detail: Be on the first shuttle. Finish your big hike by 10:30 AM, then spend the afternoon in the river or at the lodge. Temps exceed 100°F.

insiderTips.title: Day-before Angels Landing lottery works more often than you'd think.
insiderTips.detail: Apply 12:01 AM–3 PM the day before. Be flexible with your time slot.

insiderTips.title: Never skip gear rental for The Narrows.
insiderTips.detail: ~$25–32 saves your trip. "The rocks are like walking on greased bowling balls." Regular sneakers will not survive.

insiderTips.title: Kolob Canyons is the best-kept secret.
insiderTips.detail: Same $35 entry covers all Zion entrances. Same stunning beauty, a fraction of the crowds. 50 min from main canyon via I-15.

insiderTips.title: Observation Point via East Mesa > Angels Landing for views.
insiderTips.detail: No permit, no chains, no exposure. Look DOWN on Angels Landing from 2,148ft above the canyon. Arrive by 7 AM for parking.

insiderTips.title: Mid-week visits are dramatically better than weekends for crowds.
insiderTips.detail: Avoid Memorial Day and Labor Day weekends entirely.

insiderTips.title: Carry at least 3 liters of water for any major hike.
insiderTips.detail: The dry desert air "wicks moisture from your body before you even feel sweaty." Drink before you're thirsty.

insiderTips.title: Download everything before arrival.
insiderTips.detail: Cell service is spotty to non-existent on trails. Get offline maps (AllTrails, Google Maps), screenshot permits, and leave your itinerary with someone.

insiderTips.title: Watchman Trail at sunset is an underrated experience.
insiderTips.detail: Great views, rarely crowded, and the golden light on the canyon walls is stunning.

insiderTips.title: Zion is a Dark Sky Certified area.
insiderTips.detail: Stargazing is exceptional. Pa'rus Trail at night for the Milky Way. Kolob Terrace has minimal light pollution for astrophotography.

always: 3+ liters of water per person (park doesn't sell plastic bottles)
Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support
Layers — mornings are chilly even when afternoons are hot
Backpack with trail snacks (mix, bars, jerky)
seasonal: Summer: Cooling towel, electrolyte packets, extra water
Winter: Yaktrax / microspikes ($15–30), warm layers, gloves, beanie
Spring/Fall: Rain jacket, extra wind layer
angelsLanding: No trekking poles on chain section
narrows: NO cotton — wear merino, polyester, or nylon
Extra dry clothes in the car

restaurants.name: Bit & Spur Restaurant
restaurants.description: The longtime local favorite. Creative Southwestern fare: sweet potato tamales, roasted poblano mac & cheese, chicken mole. Famous prickly pear margaritas (by the pitcher). Outdoor lawn seating under fairy lights.

restaurants.name: Oscar's Cafe
restaurants.description: Crowd-pleaser for breakfast & lunch. Gigantic breakfast burritos smothered in verde sauce, huevos rancheros, garlic burger. Also known for carrot cake and full rack of ribs. Fun Tex-Mex vibe.

restaurants.name: Whiptail Grill
restaurants.description: Converted gas station. Goat cheese chile rellenos, spaghetti squash enchiladas, fish tacos, avocado salad. Good beer and wine selection. Casual and delicious.

restaurants.name: Spotted Dog Cafe
restaurants.description: Best-reviewed restaurant in Springdale. Casual American bistro with art-filled dining rooms and Euro-style sidewalk seating. Great for a sit-down dinner.

restaurants.name: MeMe's Cafe
restaurants.description: "We stop at MeMe's every time we pass through Zion." Delicious savory and sweet crepes. Wonderful service. A local gem.

restaurants.name: Zion Canyon Brew Pub
restaurants.description: Southern Utah's first microbrewery. Sits at the Virgin River's edge with sweeping red rock views. Perfect for post-hike beers.

inPark.name: Castle Dome Cafe
inPark.description: Seasonal quick-service. Coffee, pastries, burgers, beer.

lodging.name: Zion Lodge (In-Park)
lodging.description: Only lodging inside the park. Cabins with gas fireplaces and private porches. Walking distance to Emerald Pools and Grotto.

lodging.name: Cliffrose Springdale (Curio Collection by Hilton)
lodging.description: Near south entrance. Heated pool, waterfall hot tub, gardens with red rock views. Good for families (playground).

lodging.name: Cable Mountain Lodge
lodging.description: Closest hotel to south entrance. Pool, restaurant, spa, market. Premium convenience.

lodging.name: Budget Options
lodging.description: Bumbleberry Inn (central Springdale). Hurricane/La Verkin hotels (20–30 min west, significantly cheaper). Under Canvas Zion (glamping).

camping.name: Watchman Campground
camping.amenities: Flush toilets, drinking water, dump station
camping.description: Near Visitor Center and Springdale. Spring–fall dates sell out within hours of opening.
camping.tip: Loop F walk-in sites have the most privacy and shade.

camping.name: Lava Point Campground
camping.description: Remote, peaceful, at 7,900ft on Kolob Terrace Road. Good base for West Rim Trail. 10–15°F cooler than the canyon.

camping.name: South Campground
camping.closureNote: Closed for long-term rehabilitation. No confirmed reopening date. Do not plan on this for 2026.

camping.name: Sheep Bridge Road (BLM)
camping.description: Near south entrance between Hurricane and Virgin. 56 designated BLM sites with fire rings. Arrive early.

camping.name: Gooseberry Mesa (BLM)
camping.description: ~1 hour from south entrance. Tremendous canyon views. No amenities.

camping.name: Burnt Flat Gulch (BLM)
camping.description: East of park near Hwy 9. Suitable for RVs.

campingNotes.blmStayLimit: 14-day stay limit on all BLM land within a 28-day period. Bring all water and supplies.
campingNotes.showers: No showers in any NPS campground. Public showers in Springdale: $5–7 at Zion Outfitter or Sol Foods Supermarket.

items.title: Pick up Narrows rental gear
items.description: At Zion Outfitter before park entry

items.title: The Narrows (bottom-up)
items.description: First few miles to Wall Street section

items.title: The Narrows (bottom-up)
items.description: Start early, plan 6+ hours. Go to Big Spring if ambitious.

items.title: Pa'rus Trail at sunset
items.description: Or Watchman Trail for golden hour views

items.title: The Narrows (full day)
items.description: Bottom-up to Orderville Canyon or Big Spring

items.title: Observation Point or Kolob Canyons
items.description: East Mesa Trail OR Taylor Creek + Timber Creek Overlook

seasons.avoid: Mid-summer weekends, Memorial Day, Labor Day

details.description: Wildflowers (mid-April), pleasant hiking temps, waterfalls at peak flow. Narrows often closed April–May due to snowmelt. Crowds build through May. Best for Emerald Pools, Canyon Overlook.
Extreme heat + largest crowds. Monsoon season mid-July through September (flash flood risk). Start hikes before 8 AM. Narrows usually open in June. Long days.
"October is the undisputed king of Zion." Mild temps, fall colors peak late Oct, decreasing crowds, excellent Narrows conditions. Best for everything.
Solitude, snow-dusted cliffs (stunning photos). No shuttle — drive your own car up the canyon. Some trails icy (bring Yaktrax). Narrows too cold. 70% fewer visitors than June.

seasons.freeEntranceNote: As of 2026, fee-free days apply to U.S. citizens and residents only.

photography.notes: The Watchman glows red. Virgin River reflects sky. Use 5–30 sec long exposure.
Best overall canyon panorama. East Temple lit at sunrise. 0.5mi hike.
Set up 30 min early. Shuttle stop 2 (History Museum). f/9–f/14, ISO 100–200.
Short walk from shuttle stop 4. Abraham, Isaac, Jacob peaks.
270° unobstructed views. 4–6 hr RT via East Mesa.
1.75 mi in. Tripod essential, dry bag for camera. HDR imaging.
Most iconic canyon shots. Permit required.
Minimal light pollution. Best for Milky Way astrophotography.

hiddenGems.name: Kolob Canyons (NW Section)
hiddenGems.description: Physically separate from main canyon via I-15 north (Exit 40). All the beauty, none of the crowds. 2,000ft cliff walls and deep finger canyons. Taylor Creek Trail and Timber Creek Overlook are highlights. Same $35 entry covers all Zion.

hiddenGems.name: Kolob Terrace Road
hiddenGems.description: Higher elevation = 10–15°F cooler. The Subway trailhead. Northgate Peaks Trail for unique views. Excellent Milky Way photography at night. Starts in Virgin, UT.

hiddenGems.name: East Side / Mt. Carmel Hwy
hiddenGems.description: Underrated zone. Scenic switchbacks, the mile-long tunnel, Checkerboard Mesa. Canyon Overlook trailhead here (no shuttle needed). Many Pools Trail has sculpted sandstone.

hiddenGems.name: Divergent Dam
hiddenGems.description: A "true Zion secret." Crystal-clear water, secluded location ideal for swimming, sunbathing, or picnicking. Ask locally for directions.

hiddenGems.name: Cable Mountain
hiddenGems.description: 8 mi RT (from Stave Spring). Remains of a historic cable system used to transport lumber (operated 1901–1926, cables removed 1930). Historical curiosity with great views.

hiddenGems.name: Pine Creek Waterfall
hiddenGems.description: ~1 mile off-trail from a dirt pullout on Mt. Carmel Hwy, 1.5 mi north of south entrance. Crystal-clear falls with swimming in summer.

scenicDrives.name: Zion-Mt. Carmel Highway (SR-9)
scenicDrives.description: Features the 1.1-mile historic tunnel, dramatic switchbacks, and Checkerboard Mesa. Standard-size vehicles proceed through the tunnel normally.
scenicDrives.oversizedVehicleBan: Starting June 7, 2026, oversized vehicles will be banned entirely from the Zion-Mt. Carmel Tunnel.

scenicDrives.name: Kolob Canyons Road
scenicDrives.description: NW section via I-15 Exit 40. Least visited area. Timber Creek Overlook Trail at the end. No seasonal closure (may close in bad weather).

scenicDrives.name: Kolob Terrace Road
scenicDrives.description: Climbs 3,600ft to 8,100ft. Subway trailhead, Lava Point Overlook. Closed in winter.

pdfMaps.name: Zion Park Brochure & Map
pdfMaps.description: Official NPS brochure with full park map, trail locations, shuttle stops, and visitor info. The same map handed out at the entrance gate.

pdfMaps.name: Zion Wilderness Map
pdfMaps.description: Detailed backcountry/wilderness map showing all trails, campsites, and route overlaps. Essential for West Rim, Kolob Arch, Narrows top-down, and Subway planning.

pdfMaps.name: Zion Area Map
pdfMaps.description: Regional overview showing Zion Canyon, Kolob Canyons, Kolob Terrace, surrounding towns (Springdale, Hurricane, Virgin), and road connections.

apps.name: AllTrails
apps.description: Download individual trail maps for offline use. Pro version allows full offline map downloads for the entire park area. GPS tracking works without cell service. Best for trail navigation.

apps.name: Google Maps (Offline Area)
apps.description: Download the Zion area for offline use: open Google Maps > search "Zion National Park" > tap your profile > Offline Maps > Select Your Own Map. Covers roads and basic trails. Good for driving directions.

apps.name: Gaia GPS
apps.description: Serious backcountry navigation app. Topo maps, satellite imagery, public land boundaries, and trail overlays. Download entire Zion region. Best for wilderness routes and off-trail navigation.

apps.name: NPS App
apps.description: Official National Park Service app. Download the Zion park guide for offline access including maps, trail info, alerts, and ranger programs. Available on iOS and Android.

offlineChecklist: Download Zion park brochure PDF to your phone
Save offline Google Maps area covering Springdale to Mt. Carmel Junction
Download AllTrails/Gaia maps for specific trails you plan to hike
Screenshot your Angels Landing / Subway / Narrows permits
Screenshot restaurant addresses and gear rental reservations
Save the weather forecast as a screenshot the morning of your visit
Note the USGS river flow reading before entering the canyon
Download the NPS Zion app for offline alerts and info

hazards.name: Flash Floods
hazards.description: The #1 killer in Zion's slot canyons. Can occur with NO warning, even from storms miles away. Check daily flash flood potential at Visitor Center. Monsoon season (mid-Jul–Sep) is highest risk. If water rises or turns muddy: GET TO HIGH GROUND IMMEDIATELY.

hazards.name: Heat
hazards.description: Summer temps regularly exceed 100°F. Drink 1 gallon/day minimum. Start hikes before 8 AM. Heat exhaustion: nausea, dizziness, headache. Heat stroke: confusion, hot/dry skin — call 911.

hazards.name: Falling / Cliffs
hazards.description: Stay on trails and behind barriers. Wet sandstone is extremely slippery. Avoid Angels Landing chain section and Canyon Overlook if wet or icy. Rockfall is common after rain — don't linger under overhangs.

hazards.name: Wildlife
hazards.description: Mule deer and rock squirrels common — do not feed. Rattlesnakes: watch where you step/place hands. Mountain lions present but rarely seen. California condors occasionally spotted.

hazards.name: Swift Water
hazards.description: The Virgin River current can knock you off your feet. Always use a walking stick in the Narrows. Never attempt to cross swiftly flowing water. Face upstream when crossing.

hazards.name: Dehydration
hazards.description: More common than people realize. The dry desert air wicks moisture before you feel sweaty. Drink before you're thirsty. Carry electrolytes. Minimum 3L for any major hike.

gas.nearest: Shell station, 962 Zion Park Blvd (1 mi from south entrance)

practicalInfo.cellService: Spotty near Visitor Center/Lodge. None on trails. Wi-Fi at Visitor Center, Museum, Lodge.

commonMistakes.title: Not planning hikes in advance.
commonMistakes.detail: Know your trailheads, shuttle stops, and timing before you arrive. Cell service won't save you.

commonMistakes.title: Arriving mid-morning.
commonMistakes.detail: Cars wait up to 2 hours to enter the park. Be there before 8 AM or park in Springdale.

commonMistakes.title: Underestimating the altitude.
commonMistakes.detail: The lush scenery is deceptive — trails climb 1,000+ feet at elevation. You'll feel it.

commonMistakes.title: Not enough water.
commonMistakes.detail: 1 quart per 2 hours minimum, 3+ liters for major hikes. Desert air dehydrates you before you realize it.

commonMistakes.title: Skipping gear rental for The Narrows.
commonMistakes.detail: ~$25–32 for boots/socks/stick. Rocks are like "greased bowling balls." Regular sneakers = misery.

commonMistakes.title: Hiking Angels Landing at midday.
commonMistakes.detail: Dangerous congestion on narrow sections with 1,000ft drops. Go early.

commonMistakes.title: Allocating only one day.
commonMistakes.detail: One day = one major hike. Minimum two days recommended for a real experience.

commonMistakes.title: Ignoring flash flood warnings.
commonMistakes.detail: A clear sky in the canyon doesn't mean it's not pouring miles upstream. If rangers close The Narrows, they mean it.
